no_solution_leakage.reason: The hints section explicitly states 'The fix involves ensuring each call to the opener returns an independent reader' — this directly describes WHAT to change (make each call create a new reader instead of sharing one). A competent developer reading only instruction.md would know the exact fix without needing to reason about it.

solution_uniqueness_guard.reason: The f2p tests verify behavioral properties (reader independence, full content from multiple readers) rather than implementation details. Any correct fix that ensures independent readers — whether using NewReadSeekerNoOpCloserFromString, bytes.NewReader, strings.NewReader, or any other approach — would pass the tests.

tests_verify_behavior_not_text.reason: The f2p tests create Go programs that compile and execute the actual code, testing reader behavior by opening multiple readers and verifying independence. The p2p tests run `go test`, `go build`, `go vet`, `gofmt`, and `staticcheck` — all executing real processes rather than just checking file contents.

test_not_tautological.reason: The f2p tests (`test_reader_independence_f2p`, `test_opener_returns_new_reader_f2p`) exercise the actual buggy code path. Before the fix, readers share state, so reading from r2 after partially reading r1 would corrupt r1's remaining read. A stub or no-op implementation would fail because the tests verify specific byte sequences from interleaved reader operations.

behavior_in_tests.reason: The instruction requires that content adapter resources work reliably for image processing and that Hugo tests pass. The tests verify reader independence (the root cause), package tests passing, build success, and code quality — covering the stated expected outcomes.

informative_test_structure.reason: Tests are clearly named with `_f2p` and `_p2p` suffixes distinguishing fail-to-pass from pass-to-pass. Each test has a descriptive docstring explaining what it verifies and why.

structured_data_schema.reason: This task involves fixing a Go source code bug, not producing structured data output.
